subject: Add support for RKE kubernetes implementation
commit_message:
Add support for RKE kubernetes implementation

Added a new playbook rke.yml and role rke which uses rancher RKE:
https://github.com/rancher/rke

It's an implementation of the kubernetes from rancher.com folks
and it is an alternative to the official kubernetes tool: kubeadm.

NOTE: Rancher has a notion of a 'control plane' which cannot run with
etcd on all nodes in a multi-node setup. Control-plane node is the
first kubernetes node from the inventory (as of now).

Change-Id: I0bf669442a5183efa20d44fb1cac823e7ce54348
Issue-ID: OOM-1778
Signed-off-by: Petr Ospalý <p.ospaly@partner.samsung.com>
Signed-off-by: Michal Zegan <m.zegan@samsung.com>

Patch Set 7:

Hi, this change currently has a major issue that I didn't think about. Not quite sure how best to solve it in this case.
Namely: cluster.yml.j2 template will put control plane on the first kubernetes node in the kubernetes group.
However, it seems that we cannot predict which node it is. That is because although lists (like groups['kubernetes'] are ordered, dicts are not, and contents of the groups['kubernetes'] come from the inventory that is actually a dict, and dicts are not ordered.
I hit this during molecule testing, as the node2 was put before node1 in the group.
That is not only bad because it is unpredictable and does not allow doing tests on the control plane node, but you also can't predict where is your api server. If you want to access the api server externally using some form of external ip like floating ip in openstack, you are essentially not able to do that because you don't know in advance which node is your api server, it depends on python impl details.
We could either make a special group specifying control plane, or move control plane (and only it) to the infra server.
What do you think?

Patch Set 7:

I found it funny because this is basically similar to the problem with referencing index zero (no one knows what machine we are actually making special :)

I made the control plane to be on the exactly one node due to the problem with not able to have control plane together with etcd in multinode cluster (mentioned in the commit message). There are two solution: put a name on it (which everyone "argued" against it...) or we will adjust the inventory to the specifics to the rke - it is not vanilla kubernetes anyway, it is again rancher special implementation with all their lingo and technical terms - so, we can create inventory where we differentiate singlo-node vs multi-node cluster and where we designate control plane nodes, etcd backends and workers...that is what I propose.

Also I didn't know that kubernetes is modified again here. Do you have any pointers?

we found that if this playbook is executed separately, facts from k8s nodes are not collected but required by first play in rke.yml playbook. This is not acceptable and easily reproducable. The reason why it works for MichalZ is probably that he is always running site.yml.

Patch Set 10:

This patchset contains one thing that we didn't discuss.
Previously rke user was created on infra only because it *may* be useful when moving k8s components like controlplane to infra, and othervise this user was only used as an excuse to generate ssh keys that would be distributed to nodes.
However because it was decided to have a completely separate control plane group (here called kubernetes-control-plane), this argument became invalid.
For that reason what this change introduces is not to create any user on infra, but generate ssh key using ssh-keygen (the key is stored along with other cluster configs in /opt/onap/cluster).
Then, the rke_node.yml tasks are not only executed on worker nodes, but also on control plane nodes.
That way, if infra is set as control plane (or worker), it is processed like a node, the user is created and ssh key is authorized. If infra is not mentioned, this does not happen. I believe it makes far more sense.
By the way, I believe that the way it is done, you may currently be able to colocate infra, controlplane and worker on the same instance. You may also separate control plane from both infra and workers. The only thing you may not do (but it is now very easy to add) is to separate etcd's from workers.
